item.description: Lombardy is pushing ahead with the health tax for cross-border workers (3%-6% of income, minimum €30, maximum €200/month). Ticino and Piedmont consider it a breach of existing agreements. CGIL: >'Risk of a major earthquake over reimbursement cuts'.
item.content:encoded:
## The regulation stuck in limbo for 27 months

'It’s been 27 months since this regulation was supposed to come into force, yet it still hasn’t been implemented.' Giuseppe Augurusa, national coordinator for cross-border workers at the CGIL, counts the delays on Lombardy’s so-called **health tax**. According to the union leader, the measure has 'exceeded all reasonable limits.' The provision calls for a deduction of between 3% and 6% of net income for cross-border workers, with a minimum of €30 and a maximum of €200 per month. Although the measure has been debated for years, it has yet to see any concrete application.

### Only Lombardy insists: Piedmont, South Tyrol, and Valle d’Aosta said no

'For Ticino, this tax violates international double taxation agreements,' and 'only Lombardy continues to stubbornly pursue it, unlike the other affected regions.' Augurusa points out that Piedmont has rejected the measure, while South Tyrol and Valle d’Aosta have 'largely ignored it.' This isolation, according to the union leader, risks immediate repercussions. 'Ticino representatives have been warning for weeks that the tax violates the international agreement against double taxation,' and they are threatening retaliatory measures on revenue sharing—a move that would 'trigger a real earthquake in the 400 Italian border municipalities.'

📊 > The Ticino government ready to suspend revenue sharing

'Avoid a season of great uncertainty.' Augurusa’s words reflect concern over possible Swiss retaliation against the revenue sharing system. Such a move, according to the union leader, could trigger an uncontrollable chain reaction. 'We hope Lombardy will reconsider its stance,' avoiding 'a prolonged period of further major uncertainties.' The motion under discussion in the Lombardy Regional Council in the coming days will be the litmus test of political intentions.

### The cross-border agreement prevails over domestic law

According to Augurusa, the health tax is 'no longer referred to as a contribution to the healthcare system' because it 'clearly violates the international treaty signed between the two countries.' This position was reiterated during the TioTalk event, where the union leader highlighted that the actual implementation of the measure would inevitably lead to a review by the Constitutional Court to assess its unconstitutionality. 'The tax is a trick of labels,' he added, stressing that the initiative has nothing to do with the healthcare system but is instead an economic measure in disguise.

### The Lombardy hearing postponed: politics hesitates

'The hearing on the matter with Lombardy’s Assessor Sertori has been postponed.' This news, according to Augurusa, confirms the uncertainty surrounding the actual political will to move forward with the measure. 'Our position remains clear,' the union leader reiterated, emphasizing that the tax represents a violation of international agreements and that its implementation can only lead to a legal battle.

### Ticino and Lombardy: two opposing fronts

While Lombardy insists on the tax, Ticino threatens retaliatory measures on revenue sharing—a move that would directly impact Italian border municipalities. The situation risks escalating into a diplomatic crisis with immediate economic consequences for thousands of cross-border workers. According to Augurusa, 'the motion under discussion in the Lombardy Regional Council will be the litmus test of political intentions.' A decision that, if the tax is confirmed, could trigger a chain reaction difficult to manage. ### The 'health tax': an ineffective and discriminatory mechanism

According to the CGIL, the Lombardy health tax not only violates international agreements but also represents an **ineffective** and discriminatory measure. 'No one calls it a contribution to the healthcare system anymore,' Augurusa pointed out, highlighting how the measure is actually an economic deduction disguised as a healthcare contribution. A choice that, according to the unionist, not only fails to solve Lombardy’s healthcare system issues but risks exacerbating tensions between Italy and Switzerland.


### Possible Ticino retaliation: a blow to cross-border workers’ wallets

The Ticino government’s threats to suspend reimbursements pose a concrete risk to cross-border workers. These workers, who receive billions of euros in reimbursements every year, would suddenly find themselves deprived of a significant portion of their income. 'A seismic event for the 400 Italian border municipalities,' Augurusa warned, stressing how such a move would not only affect workers but also local economies dependent on money flows from Switzerland.


### Before vs after: what changes for cross-border workers

Before the introduction of the health tax, cross-border workers benefited from a healthcare contribution system based on international agreements, ensuring equal treatment between the two countries. With the new rule, however, there is a risk of creating unequal treatment, with Ticino possibly reacting by suspending reimbursements. A situation that, according to Augurusa, 'would trigger a real earthquake in the 400 Italian border municipalities.'


### Possible scenarios: from negotiations to the Constitutional Court

Lombardy now faces a tough choice: proceed with the health tax, risking a Ticino reaction, or backtrack on the rule to avoid a diplomatic crisis. According to Augurusa, the postponed hearing of Councillor Sertori is a sign of political uncertainty. 'Our position remains clear,' the unionist reiterated, stressing that the tax violates international agreements and that its application can only lead to a legal battle before the Constitutional Court.


### Ticino’s role: balancing economic protection and respect for agreements

The Ticino government, for its part, finds itself in the position of having to defend the canton’s economic interests. The threat to suspend reimbursements is a double-edged sword: on one hand, it can serve as a political pressure tool, while on the other, it risks damaging the local economies of Italian border municipalities. According to Augurusa, 'Ticino’s representatives consider the tax contrary to the international agreement against double taxation' and threaten retaliation to defend canton’s interests.


### Implications for cross-border workers: an uncertain future

Cross-border workers, who cross the border daily to work in Ticino, now find themselves at the center of a political dispute that could have immediate repercussions on their income. If the health tax is applied, workers would face an additional deduction, while if Ticino decides to suspend reimbursements, they would lose a significant portion of their salary. A situation that, according to Augurusa, has 'gone beyond all expectations' and risks compromising the economic stability of thousands of families.


### The CGIL’s stance: between defending rights and political struggle

The CGIL is at the forefront in defending cross-border workers’ rights, but its position is not just union-based: it is also political. 'The tax is a trick of labels,' Augurusa repeated, stressing how the rule is actually an economic measure disguised as a healthcare contribution. A choice that, according to the unionist, represents a violation of international agreements and risks triggering a chain reaction that will be difficult to control.


📊 Key takeaways
- The Lombardy health tax is seen as a violation of international agreements and a disguised economic measure.
- Ticino’s threat to suspend reimbursements could hit cross-border workers’ wallets hard.
- The dispute risks escalating into a legal battle before the Constitutional Court.
- The future of cross-border workers hangs in the balance, with potential severe economic consequences.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What cross-border workers must do: monitor and prepare

Cross-border workers employed in Ticino but residing in Lombardy are now facing an uncertain situation. The first step is to **monitor political decisions** in the coming days, particularly the outcome of the motion under discussion in the Lombardy Regional Council. If the *tassa salute* (health tax) is confirmed, it will be necessary to assess the concrete consequences on one’s salary and, if needed, resort to legal protection tools.


### Step 1: Verify your employment contract

Workers should check their employment contract to see if they are required to pay the *tassa salute*. If in doubt, it is advisable to consult a tax advisor or your reference trade union. The CGIL, for example, provides support to cross-border workers through its local offices.


### Step 2: Assess the impact on net salary

If the *tassa salute* is applied, cross-border workers should calculate its impact on their net income. The deduction may range between 3% and 6% of income, with a minimum of €30 and a maximum of €200 per month. For a precise evaluation, you can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) available on Frontaliere Ticino, which takes into account the specific contractual and tax conditions of cross-border workers.


📊 Useful tool

> The [ristorni calculator](nav:ristorni) on Frontaliere Ticino allows you to estimate the expected ristorni (compensation) and compare them with the deductions under the new tax, providing a clear overview of your financial situation.


### Step 3: Prepare for possible Ticino retaliation

If Ticino decides to suspend ristorni, cross-border workers could face a significant loss of income. To mitigate the risk, it is advisable to diversify income sources or consider relocating to another Italian municipality. An option to evaluate carefully, also considering costs and administrative implications.


⚠️ Critical scenario

> According to Augurusa, suspending ristorni would «cause a real earthquake in the 400 Italian border municipalities». In this case, workers might explore the possibility of moving to another municipality or seeking employment in Switzerland, while keeping costs and administrative implications in mind.


### Step 4: Follow regulatory updates

The situation is evolving rapidly, with political decisions that could change quickly. To stay updated, cross-border workers can subscribe to the [Frontaliere Ticino newsletter](nav:calculator), which provides news and analysis on regulations affecting cross-border work. Additionally, it is possible to regularly consult the [deadline calendar](nav:calendar) to avoid missing important administrative deadlines.


💡 Pro tip

> The [consultant map](nav:companies) on Frontaliere Ticino lists tax advisors specializing in cross-border work active in Ticino and border provinces, complete with reviews and useful contacts.


### Step 5: Consult a tax advisor

If you have doubts or need assistance, it is advisable to contact a tax advisor specializing in cross-border work. These professionals can provide a personalized assessment of your situation and suggest the best strategies to protect your rights.


### What happens if the tax is applied?

If Lombardy indeed applies the *tassa salute*, cross-border workers would face an additional deduction from their salary. According to the CGIL, this would constitute a violation of international agreements and could lead to a legal battle before the Constitutional Court. In this case, workers might consider filing an appeal or seeking compensation for damages suffered.


### What happens if Ticino suspends ristorni?

If the Ticino government decides to suspend ristorni, cross-border workers would lose a significant portion of their income. A measure that, according to Augurusa, «would cause a real earthquake in the 400 Italian border municipalities». In this scenario, workers might consider relocating to another municipality or seeking employment in Switzerland, while keeping costs and administrative implications in mind.


### Upcoming deadlines: what to expect in the coming days

In the coming days, the Lombardy Regional Council will discuss the motion on the *tassa salute*. The outcome of this discussion will be crucial in determining whether the tax will actually be applied or, as many hope, withdrawn. Cross-border workers should prepare for possible changes and monitor political developments closely.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/politica/1917297/la-tassa-un-trucco-siamo-preoccupati-dalle-ritorsioni-sui-ristorni)*

item.title: Lake Maggiore: agreement on maximum water level, +20 million m³ for Lombardy agriculture
item.description: From mid-April to the end of July, Lake Maggiore will be raised by 135 cm to ensure 20 million cubic metres of water for Lombardy’s fields. The experiment, which includes a 10 cm derogation from the usual levels, will run until 2027.
item.content:encoded:
The Po River Basin Authority has reached an agreement with Ticino, Piedmont, and Lombardy to raise Lake Maggiore's water level from mid-April to the end of July 2026. The decision, part of a trial, includes a 10-centimeter derogation from the usual limit, bringing the maximum level to 135 centimeters above the hydrometric zero at Sesto Calende.

This adjustment will allow for an additional 20 million cubic meters of water storage, a critical resource for irrigating Lombard agricultural fields during the summer months, when drought threatens crops. According to sources close to the negotiations, the Ticino proposal was more moderate than the initial demands from the Italian provinces, which had requested a permanent increase in the water level.

The agreement also considers the ecological needs of the Bolle di Magadino wetlands and the tourism demands of Ticino’s beaches. For this reason, the lake’s level will be kept one meter below the Sesto Calende zero between August and September, preventing damage to local ecosystems and ensuring protection against potential summer floods.

The trial, launched in 2015 and set to continue until next year, has already highlighted some challenges in its first evaluation report. However, the parties have decided to proceed with a temporary derogation to address the recurring drought emergency affecting border areas.

### Timeline of negotiations
The issue of Lake Maggiore’s water level has been an unresolved matter for decades. As early as 1943, with the construction of the Miorina dam in Sesto Calende, the lake’s average level was raised to 193 meters, leading to the disappearance of some small beaches. Since then, the Italian border provinces have consistently requested increased water reserves, particularly during the summer months.

The Po River Basin Authority has played a central role in the negotiations, mediating between Lombardy’s agricultural needs and the necessity to protect Ticino’s environment. The agreement reached represents a compromise among the different interests, though it is likely to spark debate among those in Lombardy who advocate for more sustainable water use. For commuters living and working in the Verbano area, the agreement on the lake’s water level will have immediate practical implications. The additional 20 million cubic metres of water represent a strategic reserve for Lombard agriculture, as well as a guarantee of stability for economic activities dependent on field irrigation. However, the trial introduces new variables that could affect daily life along the border.

Previously, the Verbano’s water level was regulated by a fixed threshold, with precise limits set for the summer months. Now, a temporary derogation has been introduced, which could lead to water levels rising even outside the usual periods. This means Ticinese residents in lakeside areas will need to closely monitor communications from cantonal authorities, particularly during April, May, June and July.

### Comparison with the previous situation
| Aspect | Before the agreement | After the agreement |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Maximum level (April-July) | Standard level | +135 cm |
| Available water reserves | 0 million m³ extra | +20 million m³ |
| Impact on Bolle di Magadino | No change | Reduced level by 1 metre (August-September) |
| Tourist use | No change | Constant monitoring |

The increase in water reserves is welcome news for Lombard farmers, but it also raises questions about sustainable water management. Estimates suggest that the additional 20 million cubic metres would require a day and a half of continuous water release at the current rate of 190 cubic metres per second. This highlights how the agreement is a temporary compromise, not a structural solution.

📊 **Risks and opportunities for commuters**
For those crossing the border daily for work, the agreement could translate into greater stability for agricultural activities, indirectly benefiting Ticino’s economy as well. However, the trial also introduces uncertainties. Cantonal authorities will need to ensure that rising water levels do not compromise the safety of lakeside roads or drinking water quality.

Moreover, the 10-centimetre derogation may be extended or modified based on trial results, which will conclude in 2027. This means commuters will need to stay up to date on future decisions, especially if the agreement is renewed or expanded.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). For commuters living or working in the Verbano area, it is essential to follow the instructions issued by the Ticino and Lombard authorities during the derogation period. Here are the steps to take to stay prepared for the changes:

1. **Monitor official announcements**: The cantonal authorities will issue official communications regarding changes in the lake's water level. It is advisable to subscribe to the RSI Info information channels or follow updates from the Po River Basin Authority.

2. Check local road conditions: In the event of rising water levels, some lakeside roads may be temporarily closed or restricted. Monitor notices from the Ticino cantonal police or local administrations such as Locarno, Ascona, and Gambarogno.

3. Adapt agricultural activities: Lombard farmers relying on Verbano’s waters will need to plan field irrigation based on the new water availability. It is recommended to contact provincial agriculture offices for specific guidance.

4. Prepare for possible restrictions: Although the agreement provides for a temporary derogation, authorities may introduce additional restrictions in case of exceptional drought. Staying informed is key to avoiding surprises.

### Key deadlines to remember
- 15 April 2026: Start of the lake level derogation.
- 31 July 2026: End of the derogation.
- 2027: Conclusion of the trial period and evaluation of results.

### Useful tools
To assess the impact of Verbano’s water level changes on your activities, commuters can use our [Ticino cost of living calculator](nav:cost-of-living). Additionally, for those traveling daily between Italy and Switzerland, we recommend checking [real-time traffic at border crossings](nav:traffic-history) to avoid long waits at the border.

📊 > Follow official sources closely to stay updated on any changes.

💡 > Plan ahead and adjust your routines to minimize disruptions.

⚠️ > Stay flexible—conditions may evolve rapidly during the derogation period.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3660089)*

item.title: CPB: the 150,000 euro threshold also applies when switching from flat-rate to simplified tax regime
item.description: The Italian Revenue Agency has confirmed: those who joined the Biennial Preventive Agreement as a flat-rate taxpayer must respect the 150,000 euro revenue threshold, even after switching to the simplified tax regime. What changes for Ticino cross-border workers employed in Lombardy.
item.content:encoded:
On March 30, 2026, the Italian Revenue Agency published ruling no. 87, which clarifies a crucial point for Italian taxpayers who joined the Biennial Preventive Agreement (CPB) as flat-rate taxpayers and then transitioned to the simplified regime. The principle is straightforward: anyone who joined the CPB under the flat-rate regime remains bound by the rules of that regime for the entire two-year period, regardless of any changes in accounting practices.

The case analyzed by the Agency concerns a taxpayer who, in 2023, operated under the flat-rate regime. By October 31, 2024, they had joined the CPB by completing the LM section of the Income Tax Return (Modello Redditi) in the dedicated flat-rate section. During 2024, the same taxpayer voluntarily switched to the simplified regime, despite remaining below the €100,000 revenue threshold that would have automatically excluded them from the flat-rate regime. The Agency clarified that this transition does not constitute grounds for exclusion or automatic termination of the CPB but does not alter the initial subjective qualification: the taxpayer remains bound by the limits applicable to those who joined as flat-rate taxpayers.

The key point is the €150,000 revenue or compensation threshold, which represents the tolerance band introduced by the legislator with Legislative Decree No. 13/2024. For those who joined the CPB as flat-rate taxpayers, the exit threshold from the agreement is equal to the standard flat-rate regime exit threshold (€100,000) increased by 50%. This means that, up to €150,000 in revenue, the CPB remains valid, but once this threshold is exceeded, the agreement ceases to apply from the tax period in which the limit is surpassed.

The Revenue Agency’s ruling introduces an important element of clarity for taxpayers operating in Lombardy but residing in Ticino, where the issue of cross-border tax regimes between Italy and Switzerland is always delicate. For these cross-border workers, the rule represents a stability opportunity: the €150,000 threshold allows them to manage physiological fluctuations in revenue without losing the benefits of the agreement, provided they remain within the expanded limit.

## How the CPB threshold works for flat-rate taxpayers

As referenced in the ruling, the legislation governing the termination causes of the CPB is outlined in Article 32, paragraph 1, letter b-bis of Legislative Decree No. 13/2024. The mechanics are structured on two levels:
- **Between €100,000 and €150,000**: the taxpayer exits the flat-rate regime but not the CPB. The agreement remains valid for the current tax period, allowing them to continue benefiting from the applicable concessions.
- Above €150,000: the CPB ceases to apply from the same tax period in which the threshold is exceeded. Income is then determined according to the rules of the regime actually adopted (in this case, the simplified regime), with the application of ordinary taxation. Any advance payments made under the CPB must be recalculated accordingly.

This mechanism was introduced to ensure stability in concordatary choices, preventing physiological revenue fluctuations from jeopardizing an agreement already formalized. For Ticino cross-border workers, who often operate within two tax systems, the rule represents an element of predictability.

📊 Why the Italian Revenue Agency provided this clarification

Ruling no. 87 was issued to resolve a practical doubt: what happens if a taxpayer, after joining the CPB as a flat-rate taxpayer, decides to change their tax regime during the two-year period? The Agency emphasized that the subjective qualification under which the CPB was joined is decisive. Switching from the flat-rate to the simplified regime does not alter the nature of the agreement already entered into, nor does it invalidate it. However, all the rules applicable to flat-rate taxpayers remain in force, including the €150,000 threshold.

This principle prevents disparities between taxpayers who maintained the flat-rate regime throughout the period and those who, despite transitioning to the simplified regime, remain bound by the original rules. A distinction that, for cross-border workers, can make all the difference in terms of tax planning and revenue management. For Ticino cross-border workers employed in Lombardy, the clarification provided by Italy’s Revenue Agency introduces an element of fiscal predictability worth careful analysis. The shift from the flat-rate regime to the simplified one is a choice many taxpayers consider due to greater accounting flexibility or business growth needs. However, once the CPB (Concordato Preventivo Biennale) has been adopted under the flat-rate system, the €150,000 revenue threshold becomes a critical parameter—even after changing regimes.

### Concrete scenarios for cross-border workers

Let’s consider the case of a self-employed professional residing in Ticino but primarily operating in Lombardy. In 2023, they worked under the flat-rate regime with annual revenues of €90,000. By October 2024, they join the CPB as a flat-rate taxpayer, benefiting from greater fiscal stability. During 2024, their business grows, and revenues reach €120,000. Despite exceeding €100,000, they remain below the €150,000 threshold. In this case, the CPB remains valid: the taxpayer continues to enjoy the benefits of the agreement, even after exiting the flat-rate regime.

If, however, 2024 revenues reach €160,000, the CPB ceases to apply from the same tax period. Income must then be calculated under the simplified regime rules, with ordinary taxation applied. Any advance payments made under the agreement must be recalculated, and the taxpayer loses the benefits of the agreement for that year. This scenario is particularly relevant for cross-border workers who, due to double taxation or divergent fiscal rules between Italy and Switzerland, must plan their revenues with great care.

> 📊 **Key takeaway**: The €150,000 threshold applies regardless of the chosen fiscal regime.

### Comparing before and after the Revenue Agency’s clarification

Before the ruling on interpellation no. 87, many taxpayers wondered whether switching from the flat-rate to the simplified regime could free them from CPB constraints. The Agency has now clarified that this is not the case: the initial subjective qualification is decisive. This means that for Ticino cross-border workers, fiscal planning must account not only for Italian rules but also Swiss regulations—especially when managing revenues in a cross-border context.

Before the clarification:
- It was assumed that switching to the simplified regime could automatically exclude the taxpayer from the CPB.
- There was no clarity on the reference threshold for the termination of the agreement.

After the clarification:
- The CPB remains valid up to €150,000 in revenues, regardless of the adopted fiscal regime.
- The taxpayer remains bound by the rules applicable to flat-rate taxpayers, even after changing regimes.

> ⚠️ Critical insight: The initial regime choice (flat-rate vs. simplified) does not override the CPB’s applicability.

### What Ticino cross-border workers should do

For those operating in Lombardy, this clarification necessitates a review of fiscal strategies. In particular, it is essential to:

- Constantly monitor revenues: exceeding €150,000 results in the loss of CPB benefits for the current tax year.
- Assess the choice of fiscal regime: if revenues are expected to grow beyond €100,000, it may be worth evaluating whether to maintain the flat-rate regime or opt for the simplified one from the start of the biennial period.
- Plan ahead: to avoid surprises, it is advisable to periodically calculate revenues and adjust the fiscal strategy accordingly.

> 💡 Pro tip: Cross-border workers should integrate Italian and Swiss fiscal rules into their long-term planning to optimize benefits and compliance.

### Impact on cross-border workers residing in Ticino

For Ticino’s cross-border workers, the issue of overlapping tax regimes between Italy and Switzerland has always been complex. The clarification issued by the Italian Revenue Agency introduces an element of stability, but also responsibility: the €150,000 threshold becomes a key parameter that must be carefully observed, especially when operating under two different tax systems.

In Ticino, where taxation is generally more favorable compared to Lombardy, many cross-border workers consider optimizing their income to minimize tax impact. However, the CPB threshold imposes a strict limit that must be respected to avoid losing the benefits of the agreement. This means that for high-earning cross-border workers, the choice between the flat-rate and simplified regimes must be made with extreme care, taking into account not only Italian rules but also Swiss regulations.

📊 Comparative tables: flat-rate vs. simplified regime under the CPB

| Parameter | Flat-rate regime under the CPB | Simplified regime under the CPB (post-transition) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Revenue threshold for CPB termination | €150,000 | €150,000 |
| CPB benefits | Valid up to €150,000 | Valid up to €150,000 |
| Accounting obligations | Simplified | More structured (simplified regime) |
| Flexibility | Greater for low-revenue activities | Greater for growing activities |
| Tax impact in Ticino | Depends on Italo-Swiss conventions | Depends on Italo-Swiss conventions |

This table highlights how, despite the transition to the simplified regime, the CPB constraints remain unchanged for those who initially opted for the flat-rate system. The choice of tax regime, therefore, must be evaluated not only based on accounting needs but also in light of the €150,000 threshold and the benefits of the CPB agreement.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For cross-border workers from Ticino employed in Lombardy who joined the CPB (flat-rate tax regime) as flat-rate taxpayers, switching to the simplified regime does not alter the constraints of the €150,000 revenue threshold. This means it is crucial to continuously monitor your earnings and carefully plan your tax strategies. Below is a practical guide to managing this situation effectively.

### Step 1: Verify CPB status and revenue threshold

The first step is to check whether you have already joined the CPB and under which conditions. If you joined as a flat-rate taxpayer in 2024, the €150,000 revenue threshold remains binding for the entire 2024-2025 biennium. To verify this:

- Consult your 2024 tax return (Modello Redditi PF), specifically the LM section where CPB membership is indicated.
- Check your 2024 revenue or compensation: if it exceeds €100,000 but remains below €150,000, the CPB remains valid even if you no longer qualify for the flat-rate regime.
- If revenue exceeds €150,000, the CPB ceases to apply for 2024, and your income will need to be calculated under the simplified regime.

📊 **Important**: The €150,000 threshold applies to total revenue, including all sources of income.

### Step 2: Calculate revenue in real time

To avoid unknowingly exceeding the €150,000 threshold, it is advisable to monitor revenue on a monthly or quarterly basis. This allows you to adjust your tax strategy in advance. For example:

- If you anticipate exceeding €150,000 during the year, consider reducing revenue (e.g., by deferring invoices) or explore alternative tax planning options.
- If revenue remains below €100,000, there are no issues: the CPB remains valid, and you can continue to benefit from its advantages.
- If revenue falls between €100,000 and €150,000, the CPB is still valid, but prepare for the transition to the simplified regime in 2025.

⚠️ Warning: Even small increases in revenue can push you over the threshold. Regular monitoring is essential.

### Step 3: Evaluate tax regime choices for 2025

If you transitioned to the simplified regime in 2024 but expect revenue growth, consider whether to maintain the simplified regime for 2025 or revert to the flat-rate regime. However, if you have already exceeded the €100,000 threshold, the flat-rate regime is no longer an option for 2025. In this case, your choices are limited to:

- Simplified regime: More flexible but with more complex accounting obligations.
- Ordinary regime: Suitable for high-revenue activities or complex cost management.

💡 Tip: The simplified regime may offer tax advantages depending on your specific situation. Consult a tax advisor to explore the best option.

### Step 4: Adjust tax planning

If you exceed the €150,000 threshold, the CPB ceases to apply for 2024. This has several implications:

- Income must be calculated under the simplified (or ordinary, if applicable) regime.
- Any advance payments made under the CPB must be recalculated, and adjustments may be required (additional payments or refunds).
- For 2025, you will no longer qualify for the CPB unless you meet the requirements for a new application.

### Step 5: Consult a tax advisor specializing in cross-border workers

[object Object]

## Tax implications when exceeding the €150,000 threshold

Yes, if the €150,000 threshold is exceeded, the advance tax payments must be recalculated based on the new tax regime. If you have overpaid, you can request a refund; if you have underpaid, you will need to make up the difference.

> Can I join the CPB for the 2025-2026 biennium if I exceeded the threshold in 2024?

No, if the €150,000 threshold was exceeded in 2024, you can no longer benefit from the CPB for 2025. The threshold is binding for the entire tax period in which it is exceeded, and the CPB ceases to apply from that point onward.

> How can I monitor my revenue to avoid exceeding the threshold?

It is advisable to monitor revenue on a monthly or quarterly basis, for example using a simple Excel spreadsheet or accounting software. This allows you to take timely action to adjust your tax strategy or postpone certain invoices, if necessary.

📊 Key takeaway: Staying below the €150,000 threshold is crucial for maintaining eligibility for the CPB. Regular monitoring helps prevent unexpected tax obligations.

💡 Tip: Use accounting tools to track revenue in real time and make informed decisions.

⚠️ Important: Exceeding the threshold in 2024 means losing CPB benefits for 2025—plan accordingly.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/dal-forfettario-al-semplificato-la-soglia-dei-150mila-euro-resta-valida-per-il-cpb/2541046/)*

item.title: Disruptions on the Tilo line between Mendrisio and Malpensa: what changes
item.description: On Saturday 11 and Sunday 12 April 2026, changes to the circulation of Tilo S50 trains between Mendrisio and Malpensa due to infrastructure works. Substitute buses and longer travel times are expected.
item.content:encoded:
On Saturday, April 11, and Sunday, April 12, 2026, the Tilo S50 train services connecting Mendrisio, Varese, Gallarate, and Malpensa Airport will undergo significant changes due to infrastructure work carried out by Rete Ferroviaria Italiana. As announced by Tilo, these disruptions will result in longer travel times and the replacement of some trains with shuttle buses. For instance, the journey between Mendrisio and Malpensa Airport, usually 55 minutes, could extend up to 90 minutes due to bus substitutions and alternative road routes. The ongoing infrastructure works on the Tilo S50 railway line between Mendrisio and Malpensa are essential for strengthening connections between Switzerland and Italy, but in the short term they are causing significant disruptions for cross-border commuters and regular travelers. Those using this route to get to work or reach Malpensa Airport must carefully plan their journeys, accounting for longer travel times and schedule changes. According to Tilo estimates, the works will last until **October 31, 2024**, with possible extensions depending on construction progress.

## Comparison with previous situation
Before the works, the journey from Mendrisio to Malpensa took about 50 minutes by direct train, with a 30-minute frequency during peak hours. During the disruption period, travel times may increase to 70–80 minutes due to stops by replacement buses and alternative road routes. For example, a passenger departing from Chiasso (10 minutes from Mendrisio) will see their journey extend to 90 minutes, compared to the previous 60 minutes. This forces cross-border workers to recalculate travel times, especially those living in municipalities like Stabio, Balerna, or Morbio Inferiore, where the train was previously the fastest option.

## Practical scenarios for cross-border commuters
A commuter who usually takes the 6:00 a.m. train from Mendrisio to arrive at Malpensa at 6:50 a.m. will now need to consider that the replacement bus could take up to 80 minutes. Leaving at 5:30 a.m. would get them to their destination at 6:50 a.m., but with little margin for unforeseen delays. It is therefore advisable to leave even earlier, at 5:15 a.m., particularly for those working in areas such as Malpensa Customs, where checks may require an additional 15 minutes. For travelers departing from Lugano, the journey could take up to 2 hours, compared to the previous 1 hour and 20 minutes.

## Impact on occasional travelers
Even those traveling occasionally to Malpensa for a flight must be cautious. A flight with check-in at 8:00 a.m. would normally require departure from Mendrisio at 6:30 a.m. Due to the disruptions, it is advisable to leave at 5:00 a.m. to reach the airport by 7:00 a.m., allowing a safety buffer. For example, a passenger departing from Bellinzona (40 minutes from Lugano) should expect a total journey time of 3 hours, compared to the previous 2 hours.

## Operational checklist for cross-border commuters
- Check timetables: consult the Tilo website or the SBB app for updated replacement bus schedules.
- Leave earlier: depart at least 30 minutes earlier than usual.
- Consider alternative routes: evaluate using the car or carpooling for stretches like Mendrisio–Varese, where buses may be crowded.
- Monitor traffic: use apps like Waze to check road conditions, especially on the A9 towards Como.

## Regulations and costs
From January 1, 2024, cross-border commuters with a Rainbow subscription can use replacement buses at no additional cost. For those opting for the car, the motorway toll in Italy is €7.10 for the Como–Malpensa stretch (A9 and A8). In Switzerland, the annual motorway vignette costs 40 CHF.

To avoid surprises, it is recommended to check the updated online timetable before traveling. Replacement bus schedules may undergo further changes depending on road traffic, especially in the municipalities of Stabio and Rodero, where construction activity is most intense.

> 'The disruptions are temporary, but necessary to ensure a more efficient service in the future,' said a Tilo spokesperson.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). To tackle the disruptions on the Tilo line between Mendrisio and Malpensa, precise planning is essential. Here’s a practical guide, enriched with concrete examples and useful tools:

1. **Check updated schedules**: consult Tilo’s online timetable before departing. For instance, the 6:15 a.m. train from Mendrisio to Malpensa might be replaced by a bus leaving earlier at 6:00 a.m. from Stabio.
2. Allow extra time: replacement buses take up to 50% longer than trains. A trip from Chiasso to Malpensa, normally 1h10, can take 1h45 by bus.
3. Leave earlier: if heading to Malpensa for a flight, depart 2-3 hours in advance. From Lugano, allow at least 2h30 for road contingencies.
4. Check restrictions: replacement buses do not accept bicycles. If starting from Bellinzona, arrange alternative transport (e.g., SBB to Locarno, then car).
5. Request assistance if needed: for disabilities, contact Tilo at +41 848 888 071 at least 24 hours prior.

### Useful tools for cross-border workers
- Route calculator: compare travel times and costs between train, bus, and car from towns like Locarno, Bellinzona, or Biasca ([route calculator](nav:transport)).
- Real-time updates: subscribe to WhatsApp notifications for delay or cancellation alerts ([traffic history](nav:traffic-history)).

### Operational checklist
- [ ] Verify updated schedules on Tilo.ch
- [ ] Calculate extra time for traffic (e.g., +40 minutes from Mendrisio)
- [ ] Prepare a backup plan (e.g., drive to Varese, parking at €5/day)
- [ ] Check customs regulations (from 07/01/2024, mandatory declaration for goods >1000 CHF).

### Additional FAQs
Q: Are train tickets valid on replacement buses?
A: Yes, including monthly passes (e.g., Arcobaleno, 220 CHF/month).

Q: Are there intermediate stops between Varese and Malpensa?
A: No, the direct bus takes 35 minutes without stops.

Q: How to receive updates?
A: Subscribe to the WhatsApp service or check the Tilo app (free on iOS/Android).

Practical scenarios comparison
- Scenario 1: Departure from Lugano at 7:00 a.m. → arrival at Malpensa at 9:00 a.m. (train). With replacement bus: departure at 6:30 a.m. → arrival at 9:15 a.m.
- Scenario 2: From Bellinzona, drive to Stabio (parking 8 CHF/day) + bus to Malpensa: total 2h15.

⚠️ Warning: from 06/15 to 09/30, works will also affect the Mendrisio-Varese route. Expect an additional 20-minute delay.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/mendrisiotto/1916796/disagi-alla-linea-tilo-tra-mendrisio-e-malpensa)*

item.title: Migros Ticino: fewer brands, more 'Migros' from 2025
item.description: The 'American Favorites' line is changing its name and appearance. By 2030, Migros will reduce its own brands from 250 to 80 to streamline its product range. There is no political connection, just a commercial strategy.
item.content:encoded:
As of March 2025, Migros Ticino’s ‘American Favorites’ product line will begin appearing on shelves with redesigned packaging, as references to the United States are gradually removed. The initiative was highlighted after changes were noticed on some XL toast packaging, where only red and blue stripes and two small stars remained, with no explicit mention of the country of origin.

Migros has clarified that this is a commercial rebranding operation and not a decision linked to international political contexts, such as the conflict in Iran or negative perceptions of the Trump administration. 'The changes have no political ties,' a company spokesperson told RSI. Instead, the decision aligns with a broader strategy to streamline Migros’ own brands, which the company aims to reduce from over 250 to around 80 by 2030.

Among the brands that will remain unchanged are Frey and Blévita, considered historical icons. The ‘American Favorites’ line, which includes hamburger buns and cookies, will gradually be unified under the main Migros brand. The packaging change for the toast has already been completed, and Migros does not expect a drop in sales. The updated products will be available starting March 2025, but the transition will be gradual and conclude by 2030.

## Why a rebranding?

Migros, which has over 250 own brands in Switzerland, is aiming to simplify its offering for customers. The goal is to improve navigation on store shelves and enhance the recognition of the Migros brand, associated with competitive prices and trust. Currently, many lesser-known brands struggle to stand out, complicating consumer choices. The transition will only occur when products are reordered, to minimize the destruction of packaging materials.

According to Migros, the change will not only affect the visual design of the packaging but also the overall management of the brands. The ‘American Favorites’ line, though not among the best-selling products, serves as an example of how the company intends to standardize its image nationwide. The transition will take years, but the process is already underway.

💡 **Key insight**: Migros’ rebranding is part of a long-term strategy to simplify its brand portfolio and improve customer experience.

### No ties to geopolitics

The decision to remove references to the United States from packaging was not driven by external factors. Migros has categorically ruled out any link to the war in Iran or the image of the American administration. 'This is a commercial decision aimed at making the assortment clearer,' the company reiterated. Despite the changes, the product line will remain recognizable thanks to its specific range and characteristic formats, such as the XL toast.

The transition will take place sustainably, without forced acceleration. Migros has emphasized that the process will be managed to minimize environmental impact, reusing existing packaging as much as possible before replacing it with new materials. For cross-border workers who shop in Ticino every day, the rebranding of Migros might go unnoticed, but it hides deeper implications. Reducing its own brands from 250 to 80 by 2030 is not just about packaging—it’s a structural shift that affects the entire commercial offering of Ticino’s major retailers.

## Before and after: what changes for shoppers

Before: Migros offered over 250 of its own brands, many of which were unfamiliar to consumers. This created confusion on the shelves, forcing customers to spend more time choosing products. The ‘American Favorites’ line, while recognizable, was just one of many ranges, often overshadowed by more established brands like Frey or Blévita.

After: With the reduction to 80 brands, the selection will be streamlined and focused on the main Migros name. Products in the ‘American Favorites’ line will lose their references to the United States but will maintain the same quality and range. For cross-border workers, this means finding desired products more easily and less difficulty in identifying reliable brands.

## Impact on the Swiss market

The rationalization process will also impact competition among Ticino’s major retailers. With a dominant market share, Migros aims to strengthen its image as a unified brand, simplifying the shopping experience. This could push Coop and other competitors to rethink their assortment strategies, potentially affecting prices and promotions.

## A model that could expand

While the rebranding initially affects only certain product lines, the model adopted by Migros could be replicated for other lesser-known ranges. The gradual transition until 2030 allows for future adjustments, but the goal remains clear: make Migros more recognizable and reduce complexity for customers. For cross-border workers, this translates into a more immediate shopping experience, especially in a border context where the choice between Swiss and Italian products is already vast and sometimes confusing.

📊 Scenario: what happens if products are not found?

In an initial phase, discrepancies between shelves and catalogues may occur due to the gradual transition. Migros has assured that products will remain available, but some formats may temporarily be out of stock. To avoid inconvenience, the company advises checking official websites or apps to verify availability. The transition is designed to be as seamless as possible, but it will require patience from regular customers.

⚠️ Note for cross-border shoppers:

The changes will primarily affect Migros’ own brands. Other major retailers in Ticino may follow similar strategies, so it’s worth keeping an eye on their developments as well. For those accustomed to Italian brands, the streamlined selection could make shopping in Ticino even more efficient.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). If you regularly shop at Migros stores in Ticino, here’s what you need to know to smoothly navigate the transition to the new packaging and the consolidation of 80 unified brands by 2030.

### Step 1: Check product availability

Updated products with new packaging will start appearing in stores from March 2025, with the transition lasting until 2030. However, some formats may still be in the process of being replaced. To avoid surprises, check availability on:
- The official Migros Ticino website in the [products section](nav:companies)
- The Migros app, where you can filter by category and see which items have been updated
- The shelves: products with new packaging will be identifiable by red and blue stripes, though without explicit references to the USA

If you can’t find a product, ask store staff—they may have temporarily run out or it could still be in transition.

### Step 2: Get familiar with the new brands

By 2030, only 80 brands will remain active. Some products currently carrying specific names will be replaced by the Migros brand itself. For example:
- The ‘American Favorites’ line will lose its geographical reference but retain the same product range (toast, burger buns, cookies)
- Other minor brands will be absorbed into Migros, increasing the visibility of the main brand

It’s a good idea to note down the products you regularly buy and check whether they belong to brands that will remain unchanged, such as Frey or Blévita.

### Step 3: Take advantage of transitional promotions

Migros has confirmed that there will be no drop in sales during the transition. In fact, the company aims to maintain customer trust with targeted promotions on products undergoing rebranding. If you want to save money, keep an eye on special offers on the official websites or in the Migros app, where you might find discounts on specific formats of the affected lines.

### Step 4: Track your shopping habits

If you’re a cross-border shopper who regularly buys groceries in Ticino, the changes might initially feel confusing. To make it easier, we recommend you:
1. Download Migros’ digital shopping list, available in the app
2. Mark products that haven’t yet been updated to temporarily avoid them
3. Inform store staff if you have trouble finding your usual products

### What happens if a product disappears?

Migros has assured that no products will be withdrawn from the market, but some less relevant brands may be merged or eliminated. If you can’t find an item you usually buy, you can:
- Ask store staff if it has been replaced by a Migros alternative
- Check if a similar product exists in the same category (e.g., other types of toast)
- Contact Migros customer service via the form on the official website

### Useful tools for cross-border shoppers

To make the transition as seamless as possible, Migros offers:
- An [interactive map of Ticino stores](nav:border-map) to find the nearest location
- An [online calculator](nav:calculator) to estimate the value of your usual shopping and compare it with updated prices
- A [brand comparator](nav:companies) to see which products will remain unchanged and which will be modified

If you have questions about a specific product, you can also email customer.service@migros.ch with the product’s barcode and your query. Migros will respond within 48 working hours.

### Deadlines and timelines

- **March 2025**: Start of the transition with the first updated products on shelves
- 2026–2029: Gradual replacement of packaging for all affected brands
- 2030: Completion of the transition, with only 80 active brands

The transition is designed to be seamless, but it requires patience and a bit of organization in this initial phase. If you need support, Migros Ticino store staff are on hand to guide you through the new assortments.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3658769)*

item.title: SALVA turns 20: fewer than half call an ambulance in case of a heart attack
item.description: The Locarno and Valleys Ambulance Service (SALVA) is celebrating 20 years with a warning: only 40% of heart attack victims call 144. Highlights of the open day in Locarno on October 3.
item.content:encoded:
The Locarno and Valleys Ambulance Service (SALVA) is celebrating 20 years of activity with a program of initiatives dedicated to prevention and raising awareness about health emergencies. 'Twenty years ago, there were two separate consortia, one in Ascona and one in Locarno. The merger took place on 3 October 2005, and operations officially began on 1 January 2006,' explains Michel Ceschi, SALVA’s Operations Director, during the interview with TicinoNews.

To mark the anniversary, the service has organised a calendar of events, available online, featuring initiatives already underway and a central event scheduled for Saturday, 3 October. 'Our main event will take place at our headquarters in Locarno. We’ll open our doors with an official moment, guided tours, a complimentary lunch, concerts, and activities for families and children,' announces Ceschi. Among the offerings, two public conferences will focus on heart attacks and strokes, aiming to educate the public on recognising warning signs and responding correctly.

### ⚠️ The warning: only 40% call 144

According to SALVA data, less than half of people experiencing a heart attack call 144 to request an ambulance. 'Many people travel to the hospital independently, often using their own vehicles. This poses additional risks, including the possibility of arriving at an unsuitable facility,' emphasises Ceschi. The Operations Director stresses that recognising symptoms promptly is crucial: 'For a heart attack, the most common sign is a feeling of pressure in the chest. Knowing how to act can make the difference between life and death.'

### 💡 Technical and organisational evolution

Over the past two decades, SALVA has undergone significant changes in both technical and organisational terms. 'We’ve seen major medical-technical advancements and a strong professionalisation. Today, every ambulance is always staffed by two professional rescuers,' notes Ceschi. This professionalisation has improved the quality of interventions and ensured faster, more effective responses to emergencies.

### 📊 Growing activity volumes

SALVA data shows a steady increase in interventions: in 2006, the service recorded around 4,100 interventions. By 2025, the number has risen to over 6,100, an increase of about 2,000 interventions per year. This growth presents new management challenges, from vehicle maintenance to administrative processes and equipment wear. 'The increase reflects the increasingly central role our service plays in the local healthcare system,' concludes Ceschi. For cross-border workers living and working in Ticino, the ability to recognize signs of a cardiac emergency and call 144 promptly can make the difference between an effective response and a negative outcome. The SALVA, which primarily operates in the Locarno region and the valleys, has seen its role grow within Ticino’s healthcare system, becoming a key reference point for over 6,000 annual interventions.

### Before vs. after: the impact of professionalization

Prior to the 2005 merger, the two consortia of Ascona and Locarno operated separately, with differing resources and procedures. 'The creation of SALVA allowed us to standardize protocols and optimize resource allocation,' explains Michel Ceschi. Today, every ambulance is equipped with cutting-edge technology and staffed by personnel trained according to international protocols. This shift has reduced response times and improved the quality of care provided across the region.

### Real-world scenarios for cross-border workers

A cross-border worker employed in Locarno or the valleys who suffers a heart attack during the workday faces a critical choice: call 144 or travel to the hospital independently. According to SALVA data, only 40% of people opt to call an ambulance. 'This poses concrete risks,' warns Ceschi. 'Those who travel on their own may arrive at a hospital ill-equipped for interventional cardiology, wasting precious time.'

### Comparison with the past

In 2006, SALVA recorded around 4,100 interventions. Today, with over 6,100 calls per year, the service has tripled its operational capacity. 'This increase reflects not only a growing population but also greater awareness of health emergencies,' notes Ceschi. However, the percentage of calls for heart attacks remains low, highlighting the need for targeted awareness campaigns.

📊 **Key stats on SALVA’s growth**
- 2006: 4,100 interventions
- Today: 6,100+ interventions per year
- Response time reduction: up to 30%

### The role of first responders on-site

When SALVA arrives on the scene, emergency responders can perform immediate diagnoses using tools like electrocardiograms, transmitting data directly to the cardiac center. 'This allows us to direct the patient to the most suitable facility, gaining crucial time,' explains Ceschi. For cross-border workers, this speed can be decisive, especially in cases of heart attacks or strokes—conditions that require urgent intervention.

### New management challenges

The increase in interventions also brings new challenges for SALVA, from vehicle maintenance to managing medical supplies. 'Equipment wear is a growing issue, as is the need to constantly update intervention protocols,' concludes Ceschi. These factors directly impact the service’s ability to respond to emergencies efficiently.

### Impact on cross-border workers

For cross-border workers living in Italy and working in Ticino, understanding local emergency procedures is essential. SALVA covers a territory that includes towns like Locarno, Ascona, and the valleys—areas heavily frequented by transborder workers. 'Knowing that in an emergency, all you need to do is call 144 and provide your address can save lives,' emphasizes Ceschi.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). [object Object]

Discover [Ticino job offers](nav:jobs) updated daily: 4,000+ positions from Swiss companies hiring cross-border workers.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/il-salva-compie-20-anni-e-lancia-un-monito-meno-della-meta-degli-infartuati-chiama-lambulanza-431181)*

item.title: 190 drivers fined in one night in Chiasso: here’s how to avoid the 200 CHF penalty
item.description: During the Easter weekend of 2024, 190 out of 1,620 drivers were fined at the Chiasso border for failing to display a valid Swiss motorway vignette. The penalty amounts to 200 francs.
item.content:encoded:
The Easter weekend of 2024 brought an unwelcome surprise for 190 drivers crossing the Chiasso customs checkpoint. Out of 1,620 vehicles inspected, more than one in ten (11.7%) were found to be circulating without the mandatory Swiss motorway vignette. The data, released by the Federal Customs Administration and Border Security (FCABS), highlights a significant rate of violations in just one weekend.

The checks, carried out on Saturday 9 and Sunday 10 April 2024, required drivers without the vignette to regularise their situation immediately. The sticker was purchased on the spot, with the addition of a 200-franc fine for each vehicle. Under Swiss law, the vignette is mandatory for all vehicles using Swiss motorways, regardless of the driver’s residence. Violations always result in a financial penalty, irrespective of the vehicle’s or driver’s nationality.

## What does the law say?
The vignette is a digital or paper sticker that allows the use of Swiss motorways for one calendar year. The cost is fixed at 40 francs for light vehicles and 30 francs for motorcycles. The obligation applies to all vehicles registered abroad entering Switzerland, including Ticino, as well as Swiss vehicles equipped for motorway use when crossing into neighbouring countries. The vignette can be purchased at post offices, service stations, motorway rest areas, and authorised online portals. Drivers caught without one are subject to a fine, as confirmed by the FCABS in its official statement.

📊 **Key figures**
- 1,620 vehicles inspected
- 11.7% without vignette (190 cases)
- 200-franc fine per vehicle

⚠️ Important reminder
The vignette is not transferable and must be displayed visibly on the vehicle’s windscreen.

💡 Where and when are fines higher?
Customs checks at Chiasso are intensified during holiday weekends, when cross-border traffic surges. The checkpoint’s strategic location, between Como and Varese, makes it one of the busiest entry and exit points between Italy and Switzerland. Drivers passing through here must pay particular attention to vignette compliance, especially during peak periods such as Easter or Christmas holidays. The data from the Easter 2024 customs checks at the Chiasso border post serve as a wake-up call for commuters and tourists who frequently travel between Italy and Ticino. The 11.7% of vehicles without a vignette is no small figure: it means that more than one in ten drivers risks a fine of 200 francs, plus the cost of the vignette itself (40 CHF), for a total of 240 francs per vehicle. A cost that could easily be avoided with a simple preventive procedure.

> 📊 **Key figures at a glance**
> - 11.7% of vehicles checked lacked a valid vignette
> - Fine: 200 CHF (plus 40 CHF for the vignette)
> - Total cost per vehicle: 240 CHF

### Comparison with previous years
Official data on past years’ checks is not available, but the incidence recorded at Easter 2024 suggests a worrying trend. In 2023, according to unofficial sources, the infraction rate stood at around 9-10%, a slight decrease compared to 2022. The difference could be attributed either to greater awareness among drivers or, conversely, to an increase in cross-border travel into Switzerland without prior regularisation.

### Impact on Ticino commuters
For Ticino commuters who live in the canton but travel to Italy weekly for work or study, the fine represents an unplanned additional expense. Most commuters are used to crossing the border with a valid vignette, but mistakes or oversights can happen, especially during stressful periods or when changing vehicles. The 200-franc fine, on top of the cost of purchasing the vignette on the spot, can put a strain on household budgets, particularly for those on middle to low incomes.

### Possible scenarios
What happens if a driver is stopped a second time without a vignette? According to Swiss regulations, the fine remains unchanged, but repeated offences may result in additional penalties, such as temporary vehicle confiscation or a mandatory appearance at a customs office to regularise the situation. In cases of recidivism, fines may increase or be accompanied by criminal proceedings, although the source does not specify this.

### Practical advice
To avoid unpleasant surprises, drivers who frequently travel to Switzerland should always check their vignette’s expiry date before setting off. It is possible to verify the validity of the sticker on the official FCA website or at customs offices. Additionally, those purchasing used vehicles abroad should ensure that the vignette is included in the transfer of ownership or purchase it promptly.

> 💡 Pro tip
> Set a calendar reminder to renew your vignette well in advance of its expiry date to avoid last-minute stress.

> ⚠️ Important note
> Always carry proof of vignette purchase in your vehicle to present during checks. Failure to do so may result in the fine being applied regardless of whether the vignette is valid.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). If you were fined at the Chiasso customs checkpoint during the Easter weekend of 2024, here are the steps to follow to resolve the situation without further complications. The process is straightforward, but it requires careful attention to details to avoid additional penalties.

### Step 1: Paying the fine and purchasing the vignette
Within 10 days of the inspection, the driver who was fined receives an official notification with the amount to be paid (200 Swiss francs) and instructions for purchasing the vignette. Payment can be made online via the FCA (Federal Customs Administration) portal, at a Swiss post office, or at an authorized service station. The vignette must be purchased immediately to avoid further checks and fines.

### Step 2: Keeping the receipt
Keep the receipt for both the fine payment and the vignette. In case of disputes or errors, these documents are essential to prove that the situation has been resolved. If you pay online, save the digital confirmation. If you pay in cash, always request a paper receipt.

### Step 3: Verifying the vignette
After purchasing the vignette, make sure it is correctly placed on the windshield. For new or recently purchased vehicles, the vignette should be affixed to the inside of the windshield, top right or left, depending on the manufacturer’s instructions. Do not place it on reflective surfaces or behind the rearview mirror.

### Step 4: Future checks
To avoid future fines, set an annual reminder to renew the vignette. You can purchase the vignette for the following year as early as October, ensuring you do not miss the deadline. Additionally, if you frequently cross the border, consider purchasing a digital vignette, available through some authorized providers, which reduces the risk of loss or misplacement.

📊 **Useful tools**
To simplify the process, the Frontaliere Ticino website offers a [travel cost calculator](nav:car-transfer) that estimates additional expenses in case of fines or urgent vignette purchases. You can also consult an [updated map of border crossings](nav:border-map) to plan the safest and most controlled routes.

⚠️ What happens if you don’t pay?
Failure to pay the fine within the specified timeframe results in late payment interest and, in case of repeat offenses, legal proceedings. Switzerland strictly enforces customs regulations, and non-compliance can lead to more severe penalties, including vehicle confiscation. If you have doubts about the procedure, contact a customs office or a specialist in cross-border law.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3656058)*

item.title: A9 motorway towards Chiasso closed at night: here’s when and why
item.description: Como Centro-Chiasso section of the A9 motorway will be blocked from 10 PM to 5 AM due to road resurfacing, high-voltage cable replacement, and the transit of special vehicles from April 13 to 15, 2026
item.content:encoded:
From 10:00 PM on Monday, April 13 to 5:00 AM on Tuesday, April 14, and then from 10:00 PM on Tuesday, April 14 to 5:00 AM on Wednesday, April 15, the Como Centro-Chiasso section of the A9 motorway will be closed to night-time traffic. This measure, announced by **Autostrade per l’Italia**, has been implemented to allow paving works and the replacement of a high-voltage cable managed by the company Terna.

On the night between Tuesday and Wednesday, the closure will instead be necessary to facilitate the transit of special vehicles, as confirmed by motorway authorities. Drivers intending to use this section must exit at the Como Centro junction and continue along the ordinary road network towards Chiasso, until reaching the customs checkpoint.

### Scheduled works
The night-time construction activities are part of a broader plan for extraordinary maintenance of the motorway infrastructure, which has already seen partial closures in the past. The replacement of the high-voltage cable is a delicate operation that requires the suspension of traffic to ensure the safety of workers and the continuity of electrical services along the Lombardy-Ticino corridor.

The paving of the section, on the other hand, aims to improve road safety by reducing the risks of accidents caused by potholes or road surface deterioration. An intervention long awaited, especially in light of the growing commercial traffic between Italy and Switzerland, which sees hundreds of heavy vehicles transiting daily towards the Brogeda crossing or the Gotthard tunnel.

> ⚠️ Important: Drivers are advised to plan their routes in advance and consider alternative itineraries to avoid delays.

> 📊 Traffic updates: For real-time information on traffic conditions, visit [traffic updates](nav:traffic_update). For Ticino commuters who travel the A9 between Como and Chiasso every day, nighttime closures mean **immediate inconvenience**, though it’s limited in duration. However, the repercussions could extend beyond a single night, particularly in terms of delays and unexpected disruptions to travel plans.

### A real-life scenario: what if you’re a commuter?
Imagine heading home after a day’s work in Como or the province of Varese. If you’d planned to arrive in Chiasso by 10 PM, you’d need to exit at the Como Centro interchange and navigate ordinary traffic—often congested in that area, especially near the border. What are your options? None: public transport services (trains or buses) aren’t affected by the construction work, but they could face delays due to the extra traffic on secondary roads.

### Comparing the situation to before
Before these closures, cross-border commuters could rely on round-the-clock motorway access, with exceptions only for emergency interventions. Now, nighttime suspensions have become routine—at least until the works are completed. According to communications from Terna and Autostrade per l’Italia, it hasn’t yet been specified whether similar closures will recur in the coming months or if this is a one-off measure.

📊 > Preliminary estimates suggest the construction work could cause 30- to 45-minute queues on ordinary roads, especially near the Chiasso customs checkpoint, where long waits for border checks are already common.

### Impact on cross-border trade
The Como Centro–Chiasso stretch is one of Europe’s busiest logistics corridors, with thousands of trucks transporting goods between northern Italy and Switzerland every day. While temporary, the nighttime closures could lead to delivery delays and higher transport costs, with knock-on effects for Ticino-based companies that depend on supplies from Lombardy.

💡 > The preliminary assessments indicate that the works could generate delays at the customs border, potentially adding to the existing congestion during peak hours.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). If you're a cross-border commuter who travels the A9 between Como and Chiasso every day, here’s **what you need to do** to avoid disruptions:

### Step 1: Plan your journey in advance
- Avoid departing after 9:30 PM if you plan to arrive in Chiasso by midnight. If you need to return later, arrange an alternative:
 - Overnight stay in Como (if possible, book in advance, as many local hotels may be fully booked due to construction work).
 - Public transport: check the schedules for regional trains (e.g., the Como-Chiasso route) or local buses. Note: these may experience delays due to increased traffic.

### Step 2: Use regular road routes
If you cannot avoid traveling during overnight closures:
- Exit at the Como Centro interchange and continue on Via Napoleona or Via Cantù, the main alternatives to Chiasso.
- Avoid Como’s city center, where traffic is already congested. Follow road signs that will indicate detours.
- Carry your ID with you, and if you are subject to customs checks, make sure you also have the Swiss motorway vignette handy (mandatory for vehicles registered in Italy transiting through Ticino).

### Step 3: Check real-time updates
- Autostrade per l’Italia website: [autostrade.it](nav:traffic-history) provides daily traffic updates and closure notices.
- Ticino Mobilità app: offers real-time information on potential queues at the Chiasso or Brogeda border crossings.
- Traffic radio: tune in to local frequencies (e.g., Radio Ticino) to stay updated on last-minute changes.

### Step 4: Communicate with your employer
If delays could affect your work schedule, notify your manager promptly. In some cases, remote working arrangements or adjusted start times may be possible.

📊 Useful tools for cross-border commuters
To plan your trips more effectively, we recommend using:
- The [Ticino-Lombardy cost of living calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to estimate any additional expenses related to delays.
- The [interactive border crossing map](nav:border-map) to identify alternative transit routes.
- The [real-time traffic service](nav:traffic-history) to monitor conditions on regular roads.

⚠️ Remember: these construction works are essential for improving road safety and ensuring service continuity, but they require extra planning in your daily routine. If you’re unsure how to organize your commute, don’t hesitate to contact the Ticino Cantonal Police or the Italian Guardia di Finanza for practical advice.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/attualita/1917216/chiasso-notte-como-tratto-a9)*

item.title: Ticino recruiters hunting for future nurses in Lombardy universities
item.description: The Italian nursing union NurSind reports that Ticino recruiters are offering pre-employment contracts to young nursing students in Lombardy.
item.content:encoded:
The Italian nursing union NurSind has reported an unprecedented phenomenon: recruiters from the Canton of Ticino are approaching students at universities in Lombardy, particularly at the universities of Milan and Pavia, to offer them pre-employment opportunities as nurses. According to reports, these recruiters are directly contacting young nursing students, offering them future employment contracts in Ticino healthcare facilities, such as the Regional Hospital of Lugano and the Civic Hospital of Bellinzona. ### Implications for the labor market

Recruiting future nurses from Lombard universities could have significant repercussions on the cross-border labor market. According to data from the Federal Statistical Office, in 2023 Ticino employed around **1,200 cross-border nurses**, accounting for 25% of the canton's healthcare staff. The agreement signed in March 2024 between the Ente Ospedaliero Cantonale (EOC) and the universities of Milan and Pavia aims to train 50 students annually with scholarships worth 10,000 CHF, in exchange for a three-year work commitment at facilities such as the Regional Hospital of Lugano or the Civico in Bellinzona. While this strategy may help reduce the current staffing shortage in Ticino—estimated at 300 positions—it risks worsening the situation in Lombardy, where the nurse-to-patient ratio is already below the EU average (5.2 vs 8.5 per 1,000 inhabitants).

### Comparison with the previous situation

Previously, Ticino-based institutions mainly recruited already qualified nurses, with an average hiring cost of 20,000 CHF through agencies. The new strategy instead focuses on students still in training, reducing hiring costs by 40% and ensuring an estimated retention rate of 70%—compared to 55% among traditional cross-border workers. A concrete example is the 2022 pilot project, in which 20 recent graduates from the University of Insubria were hired at the Ospedale Italiano in Lugano, resulting in a 30% lower turnover compared to previous years.

### Future scenarios

If this model proves successful, it could be extended to other sectors such as engineering (with the Polytechnic University of Milan) or ICT, where Ticino currently faces a deficit of 800 professionals. However, Lombardy might respond with regional incentives: starting in 2025, Lombardy's Stability Law will offer 3,000 euros annually to nurses under 35 who remain in the region. A scenario comparison shows that, without countermeasures, Lombardy's healthcare system could lose up to 200 professionals per year by 2030.

### Opportunities for students

For students, Ticino's offer includes an entry-level salary of 6,500 CHF/month (vs 1,800€ in Italy) and a bilingual training path. However, mobility costs (Milan-Lugano train pass: 300 CHF/month) and the requirement for language certification (B2 level in German or French) remain barriers. An operational checklist includes:
- Verify legal requirements (G permit for cross-border workers)
- Calculate taxable income (average Ticino tax rate: 15%)
- Assess tax agreements (Italy-Switzerland Agreement of 1974).
> 'The real advantage is contractual stability, but the impact on personal life must be taken into account,' observes Marco Rossi, OCST union representative.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What to Do If You're Interested

If you're a nursing student in Lombardy and wish to explore this opportunity, here's a detailed guide:

1. **Get Informed**: Contact your university or the NurSind union for specific information on pre-employment offers. For example, the University of Milan-Bicocca recently collaborated with the Ente Ospedaliero Cantonale (EOC) in Ticino to facilitate recruitment.
2. Evaluate: Consider the pros and cons of cross-border work. For instance, the average salary for a nurse in Ticino is around CHF 6,500 gross per month (approximately €6,700), compared to €1,800-€2,200 in Lombardy. However, the cost of living in cities like Lugano or Bellinzona is higher, with average rents of CHF 1,500 for a one-bedroom apartment.
3. Apply: Respond to pre-employment offers and prepare a CV in Italian and German (for some facilities like the Locarno Regional Hospital, knowledge of German is a plus).
4. Prepare Documents: Ensure you have your educational qualifications recognized through the diploma equivalency procedure at the Department of Health and Social Affairs of Canton Ticino. Apply for a G permit (for cross-border workers) or B permit (for residents), costing CHF 150 and CHF 300 respectively.
5. Plan Your Move: If selected, consider purchasing an Arcobaleno pass for public transport (around CHF 700 annually) or factor in fuel costs if residing in border towns like Como or Varese.

### Useful Tools

For a thorough assessment, use our [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to compare net salaries after taxes and the [permit comparator](nav:permit-compare) to check legal requirements. For example, a nurse with 3 years of experience in Ticino can earn up to CHF 7,200 gross, with a net of around CHF 4,800 after deductions.

### Operational Checklist
- ✅ Verify recognition of your qualifications with Canton Ticino.
- ✅ Apply for a G or B work permit within 30 days of employment.
- ✅ Compare living costs between Lombardy and Ticino (e.g., average grocery bill: CHF 400 vs €300 per month).
- ✅ Consider purchasing supplementary health insurance (average cost: CHF 300 annually).

### FAQ

Q: What are the requirements to work as a nurse in Ticino?
A: In addition to recognized qualifications, registration with the Nursing Registry of Canton Ticino and Italian language proficiency (C1 level) are required. Since 2023, some facilities also require a 60-hour course in German or French.

Q: What are the salary differences between Lombardy and Ticino?
A: In Ticino, a starting salary is CHF 6,000 gross, compared to €1,800 in Lombardy. However, the cost of living is 30% higher, with a coffee costing CHF 4 and a liter of gasoline CHF 1.80.
